title: BST Inorder Traversal
code: public void Inorder(TreeNode node, IList<int> output)

frames.caption: Traverse the BST inorder: for each node, fully visit its left subtree, then the node itself, then its right subtree.
frames.note: Depth-first traversal dives down one branch before backtracking. "Inorder" just fixes WHEN you record the current node — after the left subtree, before the right. The recursion stack does the bookkeeping for us.

frames.caption: Left subtree of 1 is finished → visit 1 and append it to the output.
frames.note: Inorder = Left, Node, Right. On a BST this emits values in SORTED order, because every value in a node's left subtree is smaller and every value in its right subtree is larger. We only "visit" a node (append it) after its entire left subtree is finished — that ordering is what makes an inorder walk the canonical O(n) way to read a BST in sorted order.

frames.caption: Left subtree of 3 is finished → visit 3 and append it to the output.
frames.note: Inorder = Left, Node, Right. On a BST this emits values in SORTED order, because every value in a node's left subtree is smaller and every value in its right subtree is larger. We only "visit" a node (append it) after its entire left subtree is finished — that ordering is what makes an inorder walk the canonical O(n) way to read a BST in sorted order.

frames.caption: Left subtree of 4 is finished → visit 4 and append it to the output.
frames.note: Inorder = Left, Node, Right. On a BST this emits values in SORTED order, because every value in a node's left subtree is smaller and every value in its right subtree is larger. We only "visit" a node (append it) after its entire left subtree is finished — that ordering is what makes an inorder walk the canonical O(n) way to read a BST in sorted order.

frames.caption: Left subtree of 5 is finished → visit 5 and append it to the output.
frames.note: Inorder = Left, Node, Right. On a BST this emits values in SORTED order, because every value in a node's left subtree is smaller and every value in its right subtree is larger. We only "visit" a node (append it) after its entire left subtree is finished — that ordering is what makes an inorder walk the canonical O(n) way to read a BST in sorted order.

frames.caption: Left subtree of 8 is finished → visit 8 and append it to the output.
frames.note: Inorder = Left, Node, Right. On a BST this emits values in SORTED order, because every value in a node's left subtree is smaller and every value in its right subtree is larger. We only "visit" a node (append it) after its entire left subtree is finished — that ordering is what makes an inorder walk the canonical O(n) way to read a BST in sorted order.

frames.caption: Left subtree of 9 is finished → visit 9 and append it to the output.
frames.note: Inorder = Left, Node, Right. On a BST this emits values in SORTED order, because every value in a node's left subtree is smaller and every value in its right subtree is larger. We only "visit" a node (append it) after its entire left subtree is finished — that ordering is what makes an inorder walk the canonical O(n) way to read a BST in sorted order.

frames.caption: Every node has been visited. The output is 1, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9 — sorted, as an inorder walk of a BST always is.
frames.note: The sorted result is not a coincidence: it is a direct consequence of the BST ordering invariant combined with the Left-Node-Right visit order. If a tree's inorder traversal is NOT sorted, it is not a valid BST — which is exactly how you validate one.
